headline: Scientists Film Goblin Shark Alive—a Living Ghost from 125 Million Years Ago
meta_description: For the first time, researchers have captured footage of a healthy goblin shark in its natural deep-sea habitat, expanding what we know about this ancient spec…
summary_short: Researchers have filmed the elusive goblin shark alive in the deep ocean for the first time, revealing it lives far deeper and wider than anyone expected.
body:
Aaron Judah had no idea what he was about to discover when colleagues mentioned archived footage from a 2019 expedition. The doctoral candidate at the University of Hawaii at Manoa decided to review the recordings anyway—and found himself staring at something scientists had never seen before: a living goblin shark, filmed naturally in the ocean.

Until now, every observation of a live goblin shark came after fishermen accidentally hooked one and brought it to the surface, where the animal would typically die within hours. This time was different. The shark appeared healthy and undisturbed, swimming freely near a seamount northwest of Jarvis Island.

The discovery triggered something even more remarkable. Colleagues from the Minderoo-UWA Deep-Sea Research Center revealed they had also filmed a goblin shark alive—in 2024, along the Tonga Trench slope. That second sighting revealed the shark living nearly 700 meters deeper than previously known possible.

These aren't just nice finds. They fundamentally change our map of where this 125-million-year-old "living fossil" actually lives. Before 2024, goblin sharks existed only in our records from narrow regions off Japan, Australia, and the western United States. Now we know they inhabit the Central Pacific too.

As Judah notes, discoveries like these remind us why exploring our deep ocean home still matters. We're only beginning to understand its secrets.
